name: Space & Astronomy
description: Explore the cosmos, from planets to black holes.

questions.text: Which planet is the largest in our solar system?
questions.explanation: Jupiter is the largest planet in our solar system, with a mass more than twice that of all other planets combined.

questions.text: What is the closest star to Earth?
questions.explanation: The Sun is the closest star to Earth, at an average distance of about 93 million miles (150 million km).

questions.text: How many moons does Mars have?
questions.explanation: Mars has two small moons named Phobos and Deimos, discovered by Asaph Hall in 1877.

questions.text: What is the name of the galaxy that contains our solar system?
questions.explanation: Our solar system is located in the Milky Way galaxy, a barred spiral galaxy with hundreds of billions of stars.

questions.text: Which planet is known as the Red Planet?
questions.explanation: Mars is called the Red Planet because its surface is covered with iron oxide (rust), giving it a reddish appearance.

questions.text: What is the name of NASA's first crewed Moon landing mission?
questions.explanation: Apollo 11 was the first crewed mission to land on the Moon on July 20, 1969, with Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin.

questions.text: Which planet has the most prominent ring system?
questions.explanation: Saturn has the most prominent and well-known ring system, composed mainly of ice particles and rocky debris.

questions.text: What is the term for the point in Earth's orbit closest to the Sun?
questions.explanation: Perihelion is the point in Earth's orbit when it is closest to the Sun, occurring around January 3 each year.

questions.text: Who was the first human to walk on the Moon?
questions.explanation: Neil Armstrong became the first human to walk on the Moon on July 20, 1969, during the Apollo 11 mission.

questions.text: What is the name of the largest moon of Saturn?
questions.explanation: Titan is Saturn's largest moon and is notable for its thick atmosphere and lakes of liquid methane.

questions.text: Approximately how long does light from the Sun take to reach Earth?
questions.explanation: Light from the Sun takes approximately 8 minutes and 20 seconds to travel the 93 million miles to Earth.

questions.text: What is the name of the first artificial satellite launched into space?
questions.explanation: Sputnik 1, launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1957, was the first artificial satellite in space.

questions.text: Which planet is often called Earth's twin due to its similar size?
questions.explanation: Venus is often called Earth's twin because it is similar in size and mass, though its surface conditions are extreme.

questions.text: What is the name of the constellation that contains the North Star (Polaris)?
questions.explanation: Polaris, the North Star, is located at the tip of the handle of Ursa Minor (the Little Dipper or Little Bear).

questions.text: How many planets are in our solar system?
questions.explanation: There are 8 planets in our solar system since Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet by the IAU in 2006.

questions.text: What is the name of the space telescope launched by NASA in 1990?
questions.explanation: The Hubble Space Telescope was launched in 1990 and has provided groundbreaking images and data about the universe.

questions.text: Which planet rotates on its side with an axial tilt of about 98 degrees?
questions.explanation: Uranus rotates on its side with an axial tilt of approximately 98 degrees, likely due to an ancient giant impact.

questions.text: What is the name of the largest volcano in the solar system?
questions.explanation: Olympus Mons on Mars is the largest volcano in the solar system, standing about 22 km (13.6 miles) high.

questions.text: What is a light-year?
options: The time light takes to circle Earth once
The distance light travels in one year
The age of a young star
questions.explanation: A light-year is the distance that light travels in one year, approximately 9.46 trillion kilometers (5.88 trillion miles).

questions.text: Which space agency launched the Mars Curiosity rover?
questions.explanation: NASA launched the Curiosity rover, which landed on Mars in August 2012 and continues to explore Gale Crater.

questions.text: What is the name of the process by which stars generate energy?
questions.explanation: Stars generate energy through nuclear fusion, fusing hydrogen atoms into helium and releasing enormous amounts of energy.

questions.text: What is the name of the point of no return around a black hole?
questions.explanation: The event horizon is the boundary around a black hole beyond which nothing, not even light, can escape its gravity.

questions.text: Which astronaut spent 340 days on the International Space Station in the One-Year Mission?
questions.explanation: Scott Kelly spent 340 days aboard the ISS from 2015 to 2016 as part of NASA's One-Year Mission study.

questions.text: What type of star is the Sun?
questions.explanation: The Sun is classified as a G-type main-sequence star, commonly known as a yellow dwarf.

questions.text: What is the name of Jupiter's largest moon?
questions.explanation: Ganymede is Jupiter's largest moon and also the largest moon in the solar system, even bigger than the planet Mercury.

questions.text: What was the name of the first space station launched by the Soviet Union?
questions.explanation: Salyut 1, launched in April 1971, was the world's first space station and was operated by the Soviet Union.

questions.text: What is the Oort Cloud?
options: A hypothetical cloud of icy objects at the solar system's edge
questions.explanation: The Oort Cloud is a hypothetical shell of icy bodies at the outer edge of the solar system, believed to be the source of long-period comets.

questions.text: What does the abbreviation ISS stand for?
questions.explanation: ISS stands for International Space Station, a habitable space station orbiting Earth jointly operated by multiple nations.

questions.text: How long does it take the Moon to orbit Earth?
questions.explanation: The Moon takes approximately 27.3 days to complete one orbit around Earth, known as a sidereal month.

questions.text: Which planet has a day longer than its year?
questions.explanation: Venus rotates so slowly that one Venusian day (243 Earth days) is longer than one Venusian year (225 Earth days).

questions.text: What is the name of the first probe to leave the solar system?
questions.explanation: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, became the first human-made object to enter interstellar space, confirmed in 2012.

questions.text: What is a neutron star?
options: A star made entirely of neutrons formed after supernova
A star in its early formation stage
A star orbiting a black hole
questions.explanation: A neutron star forms from the collapsed core of a massive star after a supernova and is composed almost entirely of neutrons.

questions.text: Which planet has the Great Dark Spot, a storm system observed by Voyager 2?
questions.explanation: Neptune's Great Dark Spot was a massive storm system observed by Voyager 2 in 1989, though it has since disappeared.

questions.text: What is the term for a star that suddenly increases in brightness due to a nuclear explosion on its surface?
questions.explanation: A nova occurs when a white dwarf in a binary system accumulates hydrogen from its companion star and ignites in a thermonuclear explosion.

questions.text: How far is the nearest star system to Earth (Alpha Centauri)?
questions.explanation: The Alpha Centauri system is approximately 4.37 light-years from Earth, making it the closest star system to our own.

questions.text: What is the Chandrasekhar limit?
options: The maximum mass of a neutron star
The maximum mass of a white dwarf (~1.4 solar masses)
The minimum temperature of a star
The maximum size of a galaxy
questions.explanation: The Chandrasekhar limit (~1.4 solar masses) is the maximum mass a white dwarf can have before gravitational collapse occurs.

questions.text: What is gravitational lensing?
options: The bending of light by gravity
Refraction of light in gas clouds
The warping of space near black holes only
questions.explanation: Gravitational lensing occurs when a massive object bends the path of light from a more distant source, acting like a lens.

questions.text: What is the name of the first black hole ever imaged by the Event Horizon Telescope in 2019?
questions.explanation: The Event Horizon Telescope captured the first image of a black hole (M87*) in the galaxy Messier 87 in April 2019.

questions.text: What is the cosmic microwave background (CMB)?
options: Thermal radiation from the early universe after the Big Bang
questions.explanation: The CMB is thermal radiation left over from the early universe about 380,000 years after the Big Bang, now cooled to ~2.7 K.

questions.text: What is a pulsar?
options: A rapidly rotating neutron star emitting beams of electromagnetic radiation
A star about to go supernova
questions.explanation: A pulsar is a rapidly rotating neutron star that emits regular pulses of electromagnetic radiation, detectable from Earth.

questions.text: What percentage of the universe is estimated to be made up of dark matter?
questions.explanation: Dark matter is estimated to make up about 27% of the universe's total mass-energy content, though it cannot be directly observed.

questions.text: What is the name of the theoretical boundary marking the edge of the observable universe?
questions.explanation: The particle horizon is the maximum distance from which light could have traveled to the observer in the age of the universe.

questions.text: Which mission first detected gravitational waves in 2015?
questions.explanation: LIGO (Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory) detected the first gravitational waves on September 14, 2015.

questions.text: What is the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram used to show?
options: The relationship between a star's luminosity and temperature
Planetary orbits in a solar system
questions.explanation: The H-R diagram plots stars by luminosity vs. surface temperature, revealing distinct groups such as main sequence, giants, and white dwarfs.

questions.text: What is the Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff (TOV) limit?
options: Maximum mass of a neutron star before collapsing into a black hole
questions.explanation: The TOV limit is the theoretical upper bound for the mass of a neutron star, beyond which it collapses into a black hole.

questions.text: What was discovered by the COBE satellite in 1992?
options: Anisotropies in the cosmic microwave background
A new class of gamma-ray bursts
questions.explanation: COBE discovered tiny temperature fluctuations (anisotropies) in the CMB, providing evidence for the seeds of galaxy formation.

questions.text: What is the Jeans instability?
options: The point at which a gas cloud begins to collapse under gravity to form stars
The maximum temperature of a red giant
The energy output of a quasar
The orbital decay of binary stars
questions.explanation: Jeans instability describes the condition under which a gas cloud's self-gravity overcomes pressure, causing it to collapse and form stars.

questions.text: Which type of galaxy has no defined shape and appears as an irregular blob of stars?
questions.explanation: Irregular galaxies lack the symmetry of elliptical or spiral galaxies and often result from gravitational interactions or mergers.

questions.text: What is Hawking radiation?
options: Theoretical thermal radiation emitted by black holes due to quantum effects
questions.explanation: Hawking radiation is theoretical thermal radiation predicted to be emitted by black holes due to quantum effects near the event horizon.

questions.text: What is the Shkadov thruster?
options: A type of ion engine for spacecraft
A theoretical stellar engine using a star's radiation pressure for propulsion
A propulsion system using dark matter
questions.explanation: A Shkadov thruster is a hypothetical megastructure (a type of stellar engine) that uses a star's own radiation pressure to slowly move the star.
